feedback.text: I urge the European Commission to address the growing concern of payment processors acting as moral gatekeepers in digital commerce. Payment processor companies are restricting purchases on platforms that host legal but in their eyes socially or politically sensitive content, which undermines consumer rights and platform autonomy. Payment processors should not have the authority to regulate or censor purchases based on vague or subjective moral standards. Concerns about content should be escalated to the platform or to the appropriate national or EU-level authorities, who are equipped to assess legality and compliance under established laws. If this trend goes unchecked, it sets a dangerous precedent. Today, payment processors may block adult content or politically sensitive material. Tomorrow, they could decide you cant buy certain food brands because theyre not green enough, or youre denied access to books, films, or art that challenges their ideologies; or even you cant purchase contraceptives because they conflict with someones moral view. This might seem hyperbolic now however allowing private financial entities to define morality and letting them enforce it will not stop if concrete actions are not taken. Such power should never rest with intermediaries whose primary role is to facilitate transactions, not adjudicate values. Digital fairness means ensuring that all actors in the digital ecosystem (users, platforms, and intermediaries) operate under clear, lawful, and democratically accountable rules. When payment processors impose arbitrary restrictions, they bypass legal systems and undermine the very principles the Digital Fairness Act seeks to uphold: equity, transparency, and trust. If Europe is to lead in digital rights, it must ensure that financial infrastructure serves the public, not polices it.
